spoken_input:
Around 5:45 PM Eastern time, the first teleconference began. Thiokol engineers were worried about low temperature and O-ring performance. Later, around 8:45 PM, the discussion resumed with charts and data about erosion, blow-by, tests, and prior experience.

This was not a casual objection made from outside the program. It came from the contractor responsible for the Solid Rocket Motors, from engineers close to the hardware, and from a record that already contained erosion, blow-by, a constraint, waivers, and temperature concern.

The charts mattered because they were the warning's last formal shape before the launch decision. They carried pieces of the history: erosion, blow-by, subscale tests, static tests, and the experience base for prior launches. But a chart can show real information and still fail if it does not make the decision standard clear.

The engineering concern was not simply that the weather felt cold. The concern was that the forecast was below the data base. The joint was being asked to work outside the experience engineers could defend.

Boisjoly focused on timing. Low temperature could affect the O-ring sealing function at the moment the joint needed to seal.

Thiokol engineers initially recommended against launch below 53 degrees Fahrenheit. That number mattered because it was tied to the lowest prior launch temperature in the relevant experience. Challenger was expected to launch far below it.

NASA managers challenged the evidence. In a technical review, challenge can be necessary. The danger came from the standard that began to govern the room.

The question drifted from "show that this is safe to fly" toward "prove that it will fail."

Engineering evidence often does not arrive as certainty. It arrives as a margin that has gone missing, a trend that has become stronger, a condition that is outside experience. If uncertainty blocks launch, the warning has force. If uncertainty permits launch, the warning has to do something much harder: predict catastrophe in advance.

The engineers had evidence of risk. They did not have a precise equation that converted temperature into certain loss of vehicle. That absence became central. The uncertainty that should have made launch harder instead made the no-launch case easier to challenge.

previous_text:
h, but it gave cold decision-level weight.

The way the data was framed mattered. If the room looks only at flights where distress already appeared, the temperature pattern can seem debatable. If the room sees the full flight history, including flights with no distress, the cold-weather signal becomes harder to treat as noise. The framing changed what kind of warning the data became.

Feynman's appendix named the broader danger: a risk flown before without catastrophe can start to look safer than it is.

A survived flight can show that the vehicle got through while saying little about whether the risk is safe to repeat.

By January 1986, the program had evidence of damage, a temperature concern, a launch constraint, repeated waivers, a warning memo, and a pattern that was easier to see when the full history was placed in one frame.

By January 1986, the warning was part of the way the program kept flying.

On January 27, the forecast turned the old joint problem into a launch decision.
next_text:
Lund later described the logic plainly. They got into the mode of trying to prove the motor would not work, and they could not prove absolute failure. That gap helped the launch case survive.

Thiokol management took a caucus. Inside that process came the phrase that still carries the decision's shape: take off the engineering hat and put on the management hat.

The same cold existed. The same joint existed. The same data limits existed. But the judgment changed categories.

Around 11 p.m., Thiokol reversed and recommended launch.

The final recommendation looked like process: a contractor position, charts, managers, and a conclusion. It no longer carried the engineering refusal that made the warning operational.

No single phrase or person explains the disaster. The meeting chain could take a specific engineering objection and return a launch recommendation from the same process.

The launch-eve reversal is dramatic, but it is not the whole system failure.
